whoUses: everyone; the polite substitute for merde
usageContext: safe everywhere, including in front of children and bosses

whoUses: practically everyone, in practically every informal context
usageContext: frustration, surprise, emphasis. So integrated it barely registers among adults — but still vulgar on paper. Also 'good luck' in the theater world ('je te dis merde !').
warningNotes: Fine among friends; avoid in job interviews and first meetings with in-laws.

whoUses: extremely widespread; the most-said French swear word
usageContext: detached from its literal meaning — pure exclamation of anger, awe, joy, emphasis: 'putain, c'est beau !'. In the South, practically punctuation ('putaing con').
warningNotes: Never use it literally about a person — as an insult directed at someone it becomes severity 6+. The exclamation and the insult are different words that happen to be spelled the same.

usageContext: 'quel con !' (what an idiot), 'c'est con' (that's dumb/that sucks), 'fais pas le con' (don't do anything stupid). Between close friends can even be affectionate.
warningNotes: Calling a stranger 'connard/connasse' (the amplified form) is fight-starting territory — severity jumps to 5-6.

examples.text: C'est con, on a raté le début.

natural: it pisses me off / what a pain
usageContext: frustration with situations: 'ça me fait chier d'y aller' (I really can't be bothered). 'Fait chier !' alone = 'damn it!'. Adjective: 'chiant' (annoying) — vulgar but constant.
warningNotes: 'Tu me fais chier' aimed AT someone is aggressive — keep it about situations, not people.

usageContext: 'quel bordel !' (what a mess!), 'c'est le bordel' (it's chaos), and as an intensifying exclamation, often stacked: 'putain de bordel de merde' for full despair

examples.text: C'est le bordel dans ma chambre.
examples.translation: My room is a total mess.

usageContext: genuinely aggressive to strangers; among very close friends, mock-offended 'oh, ta gueule' can be banter. 'Ferme-la' is a notch softer.
warningNotes: As a learner you lack the intonation control to land this as banter — you will just sound hostile. Recognize it, don't deploy it.

examples.text: Oh ta gueule, toi, tu dis n'importe quoi !
examples.translation: Oh shut it, you're talking nonsense! (between close friends, laughing)

whoUses: heard in anger and in fiction
usageContext: a real insult about someone's character: 'quel salaud !' (what a bastard). The feminine 'salope' is significantly more degrading and sexualized than the masculine.
warningNotes: The masculine/feminine asymmetry matters: 'salope' carries misogynist weight 'salaud' doesn't. A learner has no good reason to produce either — but you'll hear both in films constantly.

examples.text: Il est parti sans payer, le salaud !
examples.translation: He left without paying, the bastard!
